en.extract:
The brown-cheeked fulvetta (Alcippe poioicephala) is a babbler-like bird belonging to the family Leiothrichidae found in the forests of South and Southeast Asia. It was formerly called the quaker babbler in India and common nun babbler in Malaya. They forage on trees for insects, sometimes hanging from the branches in a tit-like manner, and visiting flowers for nectar. They are often hard to see in vegetation but they have loud multi-note whistling calls in the morning and afternoon.


Description

The brown-cheeked fulvetta is a small dull coloured bird measuring 15 cm from beak to tail tip. The grey bill is short and weak and the upper mandible is curved. The genus Alcippe has the bill widest at the nostrils which are long and has a membrane overhanging it. The tail feathers are all nearly of the same length. The wings are brownish, the underside is buff and the crown is grey. The crown is not demarcated from the rest of the head in the nominate subspecies but in some, there is a dark stripe that begins behind the eyes and meets at the nape. The sexes are indistinguishable and several birds may forage in a loose flock, sometimes joining mixed-species foraging flocks. While foraging, they are often tit-like, dangling below branches while searching for insect prey. Their calls especially during dawn are loud and can be mistaken for that of a magpie robin or puff-throated babbler.


Distribution and habitat
The brown-cheeked fulvetta is a resident breeding bird of moist deciduous and evergreen forests particularly in the hills. They are distributed widely in peninsular India, with other populations to the east of the Brahmaputra river and extending into Southeast Asia.


Taxonomy

The species was described by Jerdon in the Madras Journal of Literature and Science in 1844 (but technically published in 1841) who placed the species within the broad genus Timalia and called it the small grey-headed babbler. His type specimen came from the Coonoor ghats. The bird was placed with the babblers in the subfamily Timaliinae for a significant period. The species was placed in the genus Alcippe by Blyth who created the genus in 1844. Oberholser created the genus Alcippornis as a substitute name in 1905 but this was based on a misunderstanding of the type species. The name Quaker (and sometimes nun babbler) babbler was used for the species when it was considered as a babbler. The family Timaliidae was considered a dustbin taxon for a long time. A series of molecular phylogeny studies of the babblers resulted in the splitting of the group into several clades including several family level splits. When the group was separated from the traditional babblers it was called a fulvetta along with several other species. Further refining resulted in the separation from the majority of fulvettas but the common name has been retained.  
Within its range, several subspecies have been named which vary in plumage (another Alcippe species has also been found to have substantial geographical variation through isolation and evolutionary history), the brown cheek is seen more prominently in some of the Southeast Asian populations: 

A. p. poioicephala (Jerdon, 1844[1841]) - Western Ghats and southern peninsular India (includes A. p. brucei described from Mahabaleshwar which is used for the birds of the Eastern Ghats and central India. A. p. bourdilloni from Travancore described by Hume not recognized)
A. p. haringtoniae Hartert, 1909 - E Myanmar - has darkish subcoronal stripes on the head
A. p. phayrei Blyth, 1845 - S W Myanmar
A. p. fusca Godwin-Austen, 1876 - E Bangladesh
A. p. alearis  (Bangs and Van Tyne, 1930) - S China (S Yunnan), N and C Laos, N Vietnam (W Tonkin) and N Thailand
A. p. karenni  Robinson and Kloss, 1928 - SE and S Myanmar (S to N Tenasserim) and adjacent W Thailand - (replaces the names magnirostris Walden, 1875 and blythi  Collin and Hartert, 1927)
A. p. davisoni Harington, 1915 - extreme S Myanmar (S Tenasserim) and N peninsular Thailand


Ecology and behaviour
The fulvetta has been suspected to have two broods in a year and nests have been observed from January to June with a peak in January–February. A study in southern India found 38 nests within 50,000 square metres. The nest is a cup, built with green moss, rootlets, lichen, leaves, and grass lined with rootlets and placed in a fork or suspended from the twigs at a mean height of 68.21 cm from ground. Mean nest width was 91.8 mm and depth 48.7 mm. Clutch size was two to three eggs and the incubation period is 10 ± 2 days and the nestling period is 12 ± 2 days. Hatching success was 55% while the nestling success was 32%. The most preferred plants for nesting were shrubs of the species Lasianthus ciliatus (36%) followed by the Saprosma fragrans (27%) and Thottea siliquosa (23%).
With their loud calls, it has been suggested that fulvettas have a role in the organization of mixed species foraging flocks and have been noted as being dominant members of flocks in Malayan forests.
The species visits flowers for nectar and has been recorded visiting the flowers of Erythrina and Cullenia exarillata. The ectoparasitic fly Ornithoctona australasiae (Fabricius, 1805) has been collected on brown-cheeked fulvettas from Malaya. A species of parasitic cestode, Dicranotaenia alcippina, has been described from the species.


References


Bibliography
Collar, N. J. & Robson C. 2007. Family Timaliidae (Babblers)  pp. 70 – 291 in; del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A. & Christie, D.A. eds. Handbook of the Birds of the World, Vol. 12. Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona.
Grimmett, Richard; Inskipp, Carol, Inskipp, Tim & Byers, Clive (1999): Birds of India, Pakistan, Nepal, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Sri Lanka, and the Maldives. Princeton University Press, Princeton, N.J.. ISBN 0-691-04910-6

The '''brown-cheeked fulvetta''' ('''''Alcippe poioicephala''''') is a babbler-like bird belonging to the family [[Leiothrichidae]] found in the forests of South and Southeast Asia. It was formerly called the '''quaker babbler''' in India and '''common nun babbler''' in Malaya. They forage on trees for insects, sometimes hanging from the branches in a tit-like manner, and visiting flowers for nectar. They are often hard to see in vegetation but they have loud multi-note whistling calls in the morning and afternoon.

== Description ==
[[File:Brown-cheeked fulvetta call recorded in February 2011 at Agumbe, Karnataka.wav|thumb|left|Calls of ''A. p. poioicephala'' from the Western Ghats]]
The brown-cheeked fulvetta is a small dull coloured bird measuring 15 cm from beak to tail tip. The grey bill is short and weak and the upper mandible is curved. The genus ''Alcippe'' has the bill widest at the nostrils which are long and has a membrane overhanging it. The tail feathers are all nearly of the same length.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{cite book |author=Baker, E.C. Stuart |url=https://archive.org/details/birds01bakeiala/page/274/mode/2up |title=The Fauna of British India, including Ceylon and Burma. Birds. Volume I. |publisher=Taylor and Francis |year=1922 |place=London |pages=277–280}}</ref> The wings are brownish, the underside is buff and the crown is grey. The crown is not demarcated from the rest of the head in the nominate subspecies but in some, there is a dark stripe that begins behind the eyes and meets at the nape. The sexes are indistinguishable and several birds may forage in a loose flock, sometimes joining mixed-species foraging flocks.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sridhar |first1=Hari |last2=Jordán |first2=Ferenc |last3=Shanker |first3=Kartik |date=2013 |title=Species importance in a heterospecific foraging association network |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1600-0706.2013.00101.x |journal=Oikos |language=en |volume=122 |issue=9 |pages=1325–1334 |doi=10.1111/j.1600-0706.2013.00101.x |bibcode=2013Oikos.122.1325S |issn=0030-1299}}</ref> While foraging, they are often tit-like, dangling below branches while searching for insect prey. Their calls especially during dawn are loud and can be mistaken for that of a magpie robin or [[puff-throated babbler]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Whistler, Hugh |url=https://archive.org/details/dli.pahar.3233/page/55/mode/1up |title=Popular handbook of Indian Birds |publisher=Oliver and Boyd |year=1949 |edition=4 |pages=54–55}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The brown-cheeked fulvetta is a resident breeding bird of moist deciduous and evergreen forests particularly in the hills. They are distributed widely in peninsular India, with other populations to the east of the Brahmaputra river and extending into Southeast Asia.

== Taxonomy ==
[[File:Brown cheeked fulvetta.jpg|thumb|left|''A. p. poioicephala'' from the Western Ghats]]
The species was described by Jerdon in the ''Madras Journal of Literature and Science'' in 1844 (but technically published in 1841) who placed the species within the broad genus ''Timalia'' and called it the small [[grey-headed babbler]]. His type specimen came from the Coonoor ghats.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Jerdon, T.C. |date=1844  |title=Supplement to the Catalogue of Birds of the Peninsula of India |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/46472574 |journal=Madras Journal of Literature and Science |volume=13 |pages=156–174}}</ref> The bird was placed with the babblers in the subfamily Timaliinae for a significant period. The species was placed in the genus ''Alcippe'' by Blyth who created the genus in 1844.<ref>{{cite journal|url= https://archive.org/details/dli.calcutta.00011/page/369/mode/2up|title=Appendix to Mr Blyth's Report for December Meeting, 1842|pages=361–395|journal=Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal|volume=13|author=Blyth, E.|date=1844 }}</ref> Oberholser created the genus ''Alcippornis'' as a substitute name in 1905 but this was based on a misunderstanding of the type species.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Riley, J. H. |year=1933 |title=The names of two genera of Timaline birds |journal=Auk |volume=50 |issue=3 |pages=363–364|doi=10.2307/4076668 |jstor=4076668 }}</ref> The name Quaker (and sometimes nun babbler) babbler was used for the species when it was considered as a babbler. The family Timaliidae was considered a [[Wastebasket taxon|dustbin taxon]] for a long time.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gelang |first1=Magnus |last2=Cibois |first2=Alice |last3=Pasquet |first3=Eric |last4=Olsson |first4=Urban |last5=Alström |first5=Per |last6=Ericson |first6=Per G. P |date=2009 |title=Phylogeny of babblers (Aves, Passeriformes): major lineages, family limits and classification |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1463-6409.2008.00374.x |journal=Zoologica Scripta |language=en |volume=38 |issue=3 |pages=225–236 |doi=10.1111/j.1463-6409.2008.00374.x |issn=0300-3256|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name=":0" /> A series of molecular phylogeny studies of the babblers resulted in the splitting of the group into several clades including several family level splits. When the group was separated from the traditional babblers it was called a fulvetta along with several other species.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pasquet |first1=Eric |last2=Bourdon |first2=Estelle |last3=Kalyakin |first3=Mikhail V. |last4=Cibois |first4=Alice |date=2006 |title=The fulvettas ( Alcippe , Timaliidae, Aves): a polyphyletic group |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1463-6409.2006.00253.x |journal=Zoologica Scripta |language=en |volume=35 |issue=6 |pages=559–566 |doi=10.1111/j.1463-6409.2006.00253.x |issn=0300-3256|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Further refining resulted in the separation from the majority of fulvettas but the common name has been retained.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cai |first1=Tianlong |last2=Shao |first2=Shimiao |last3=Kennedy |first3=Jonathan D. |last4=Alström |first4=Per |last5=Moyle |first5=Robert G. |last6=Qu |first6=Yanhua |last7=Lei |first7=Fumin |last8=Fjeldså |first8=Jon |date=2020 |title=The role of evolutionary time, diversification rates and dispersal in determining the global diversity of a large radiation of passerine birds |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jbi.13823 |journal=Journal of Biogeography |language=en |volume=47 |issue=7 |pages=1612–1625 |doi=10.1111/jbi.13823 |bibcode=2020JBiog..47.1612C |issn=0305-0270|url-access=subscription }}</ref>  

Within its range, several subspecies have been named which vary in plumage (another ''Alcippe'' species has also been found to have substantial geographical variation through isolation and evolutionary history<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Zou |first1=Fasheng |last2=Chuan Lim |first2=Haw |last3=Marks |first3=Ben D. |last4=Moyle |first4=Robert G. |last5=Sheldon |first5=Frederick H. |date=2007 |title=Molecular phylogenetic analysis of the Grey-cheeked Fulvetta (Alcippe morrisonia) of China and Indochina: A case of remarkable genetic divergence in a "species" |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1055790306004969 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |language=en |volume=44 |issue=1 |pages=165–174 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.004|pmid=17300964 |bibcode=2007MolPE..44..165Z |url-access=subscription }}</ref>), the brown cheek is seen more prominently in some of the Southeast Asian populations:<ref>{{cite journal |author=Ticehurst, C.B. |year=1930 |title=[A. n. stanfordi, susbsp. nov.] |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40498435 |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |volume=50 |pages=84–85}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|url= https://archive.org/details/checklistofbirds101964pete/page/406/mode/2up|title=Check-list of birds of the world. Volume 10|pages=407–408|editor1=Mayr, E.|editor2=Paynter, Jr., R. A.|place= Cambridge, MA|year=1964| publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology}}</ref> 

* ''A. p. poioicephala'' (Jerdon, 1844[1841]) - [[Western Ghats]] and southern peninsular India (includes ''A. p. brucei'' described from Mahabaleshwar<ref>{{cite journal |author=Hume, A.O. |year=1870 |title=Additional observations regarding some species of birds noticed by Mr. W.T. Blanford, in his "Ornithological notes from southern, western and central India" |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41301698 |journal=Jour. Asiatic Soc. Bengal |volume=39 |issue=2 |pages=113–122}}</ref> which is used for the birds of the [[Eastern Ghats]] and central India. ''A. p. bourdilloni'' from Travancore described by Hume not recognized)
* ''A. p. haringtoniae'' {{small|Hartert, 1909}} - E Myanmar - has darkish subcoronal stripes on the head<ref name=":0">{{cite journal|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/62554172|title=Notes on Indian Timeliides and their allies|pages=417–453|author=Harington, H.H.|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=23|year=1915|author-link=Herbert Hastings Harington}}</ref>
* ''A. p. phayrei'' {{small|Blyth, 1845}} - S W Myanmar
* ''A. p. fusca'' {{small|Godwin-Austen, 1876}} - E Bangladesh<ref>{{cite journal |author=Godwin-Austen, H H |year=1876 |title=Fifth list of birds from the hill ranges of the north-east frontier of India. |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/37118210 |journal=Jour. Asiatic Soc. Bengal |volume=45 |issue=2 |pages=191–204}}</ref>
* ''A. p. alearis''  {{small|(Bangs and Van Tyne, 1930}}) - S China (S Yunnan), N and C Laos, N Vietnam (W Tonkin) and N Thailand<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Bangs, Outram |author2=Van Tyne, Josselyn |year=1930 |title=Descriptions of five new Indo-Chinese birds |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2755073 |journal=Field Museum of Natural History |volume=18 |issue=1}}</ref>
* ''A. p. karenni'' {{small| Robinson and Kloss, 1928}} - SE and S Myanmar (S to N Tenasserim) and adjacent W Thailand - (replaces the names ''magnirostris'' Walden, 1875 and ''blythi''  Collin and Hartert, 1927<ref>{{cite journal|url=https://archive.org/details/novitateszoologi34lond/page/50/mode/1up|title=Nomina mutanda|author1=Collin, A.|author2=Hartert, E.|year=1927| journal=Novitates Zoologicae|volume=34|pages=50–52|doi=10.5962/bhl.part.11783 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Robinson, H.C. |author2=Kloss, C. Boden |year=1923 |title=Some remarks on Mr C. Stuart Baker's new volume on the Birds in the "Fauna of British India" |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/31810921 |journal=Journal and Proceedings of the Asiatic Society of Bengal |pages=559–569|volume=18}}</ref>)
* ''A. p. davisoni'' {{small|Harington, 1915}} - extreme S Myanmar (S Tenasserim) and N peninsular Thailand

==Ecology and behaviour==
The fulvetta has been suspected to have two broods in a year and nests have been observed from January to June with a peak in January–February. A study in southern India found 38 nests within 50,000 square metres. The [[nest]] is a cup, built with green moss, rootlets, lichen, leaves, and grass lined with rootlets and placed in a fork or suspended from the twigs at a mean height of 68.21 cm from ground. Mean nest width was 91.8 mm and depth 48.7 mm. Clutch size was two to three eggs and the [[incubation period]] is 10 ± 2 days and the nestling period is 12 ± 2 days. Hatching success was 55% while the nestling success was 32%. The most preferred plants for nesting were shrubs of the species ''[[Lasianthus ciliatus]]'' (36%) followed by the ''[[Saprosma fragrans]]'' (27%) and ''[[Thottea siliquosa]]'' (23%).<ref>{{cite journal |title=Brown-cheeked Fulvetta |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/brcful1/cur/introduction |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |doi=10.2173/bow.brcful1.01 |s2cid=241610200 |access-date=31 July 2021|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

With their loud calls, it has been suggested that fulvettas have a role in the organization of [[Mixed-species foraging flock|mixed species foraging flocks]] and have been noted as being dominant members of flocks in Malayan forests.<ref>{{cite journal |author=McClure, H Elliott |author-link=Elliott McClure |year=1967 |title=The Composition of Mixed Species Flocks in Lowland and Sub-Montane Forests of Malaya |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/52208814 |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=79 |issue=2 |pages=131–154}}</ref>

The species visits flowers for nectar and has been recorded visiting the flowers of ''[[Erythrina]]'' and ''[[Cullenia exarillata]].''<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Atluri |first1=J. B. |last2=Rao |first2=S. Purnachandra |last3=Reddi |first3=C. Subba |date=2000 |title=Pollination ecology of Helicteres isora Linn. (Sterculiaceae) |journal=Current Science |volume=78 |issue=6 |pages=713–718 |jstor=24103887 |issn=0011-3891}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Ganesh, T. |author2=Devy, M. S. |year=2000 |title=Bird, flowers and pollination ecology |journal=Current Science |volume=79 |issue=6 |page=689}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Ganesh, T. |author2=Devy, M. Soubadra |year=2000 |title=Flower use by arboreal mammals and pollination of a rainforest tree in Southwestern Ghats, India |journal=Selbyana |volume=21 |pages=60–65}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Ganesh, T |author2=Davidar, P |year=1997 |title=Flowering phenology and flower predation of ''Cullenia exarillata'' (Bombacaceae) by arboreal vertebrates in Western Ghats, India. |journal=Jour. Tropical Ecol. |volume=13 |issue=3 |pages=459–468|doi=10.1017/S0266467400010622 }}</ref> The ectoparasitic fly ''[[Ornithoctona australasiae]]'' (Fabricius, 1805) has been collected on brown-cheeked fulvettas from Malaya.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Maa, T.C. |year=1969 |title=Synopses of the genera ''Ornitholophila'' and ''Ornithoctona'' with remarks on their habitat diversification (Diptera: Hippoboscidae |url=https://archive.org/details/pacific-insects-monograph-20-001/mode/1up |journal=Pacific Insects Monograph |volume=20 |pages=1–23}}</ref> A species of parasitic cestode, ''Dicranotaenia alcippina'', has been described from the species.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Srivastava, A. K. |author2=Capoor, V. N. |year=1975 |title=On a new cestode Dicranotaenia alcippina N. sp. |journal=Proceedings of the 62nd Indian Science Congress, Delhi. Part III, Group B. |page=210}}</ref>

*Grimmett, Richard; Inskipp, Carol, Inskipp, Tim & Byers, Clive (1999): ''Birds of India, Pakistan, Nepal, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Sri Lanka, and the Maldives''. Princeton University Press, Princeton, N.J.. <small>{{ISBN|0-691-04910-6}}</small>

zh.extract:
褐脸雀鹛（学名：Alcippe poioicephala），是雀眉科雀鹛属的一种，分布于缅甸、孟加拉国、老挝、中国大陆、越南、印度和泰国。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
褐脸雀鹛的平均体重约为19.4克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的湿润低地林、亚热带或热带的旱林、亚热带或热带的高海拔疏灌丛、亚热带或热带的（低地）湿润疏灌丛和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。该物种的模式产地在印度Nilgiris。


亚种
褐脸雀鹛滇南亚种（学名：Alcippe poioicephala alearis）。分布于老挝、越南、泰国以及中国大陆的云南等地。该物种的模式产地在越南北部Muongoun。
褐脸雀鹛滇西亚种（学名：Alcippe poioicephala haringtoniae）。分布于缅甸、泰国以及中国大陆的云南等地。该物种的模式产地在缅甸八莫。


参考文献
